**DRAFT - competitive intelligence generated from internal Ericsson sources, anonymised. Customer not named. Review before external use.**

**Competitive Intelligence - Nokia**

**Profile and position**

Nokia is a global, publicly traded telecom vendor founded in the 19th century and headquartered in Espoo, with about 85 000 employees and operations in roughly 100 countries. It is one of the leading vendors in mobile networks with strong RAN domain expertise and a broad portfolio of network functions, automation, security products and professional services.

From 2026 Nokia operates two main business segments: **Network Infrastructure** as the growth engine for optical, IP and fixed networks, and **Mobile Infrastructure** combining core, radio and technology standards, plus a **Portfolio Businesses** bucket for non-core assets under strategic review. The company has pivoted its strategy to "lead the AI-driven transformation of networks," adding a central Technology and AI Organisation and a Corporate Development Organisation and implementing a unified "One Nokia" customer interface across Network and Mobile Infrastructure.

Internal market trackers show Nokia with a 5G RAN market share in the high-teens versus Ericsson in the mid-twenties globally, with Ericsson ahead in EMEA and especially in Middle East and Africa. Nokia's RAN position in EMEA is material but structurally smaller than Ericsson's, which shapes its more challenger-oriented commercial posture in the region.

**Portfolio and technical position**

Nokia has strong **RAN and cloud RAN** capabilities. It has moved away from proprietary cloud hardware and now focuses on RAN cloud-native network functions and management and automation layers, offering pre-integrated architectures based on Red Hat OpenShift and Dell or HPE servers with Cloud RAN SmartNIC, while also supporting deployment on major public clouds such as AWS, Google Cloud, IBM and Microsoft. Its RAN CU/DU functions run on AMD, Arm and Intel CPUs and it can act as primary integrator for vRAN and Open RAN deployments with end-to-end design, integration, lifecycle management and training services.

Nokia's **anyRAN** and Open RAN strategy is technically credible and highly visible. It is a long-standing tier-1 advocate of O-RAN, drove adoption of the eCPRI 7-2x fronthaul interface and uplink performance improvements, and co-chairs key O-RAN Alliance work groups on Open Fronthaul, near-RT RIC and O&M. It has commercially deployed multi-supplier O-RAN with third-party radios in large CSP networks in Europe and Japan and runs advanced multi-supplier testbeds in Dallas and Ulm as well as OTIC-aligned labs. Nokia's roadmap phases in O-RUs, Cloud RAN and O-RAN Massive MIMO, targeting feature and performance parity across purpose-built and cloud RAN.

At the same time, independent analysis notes that Nokia has not yet publicly announced commercial deployments where it carries the leading role in integrating and supporting the full RAN cloud platform, so vRAN/Open RAN traction as prime integrator remains thinner than the marketing suggests.

In **AI in the RAN**, Nokia is advanced. Its AI-RAN strategy is anchored in anyRAN and a strategic partnership with NVIDIA, which invests about USD 1 billion and co-develops the ARC-Pro platform, a 6G-ready accelerated computing system that runs 5G/6G RAN software on programmable COTS hardware. anyRAN supports three deployment paths: GPU cards in existing AirScale baseband, D-RAN vRAN on COTS servers at sites and centralized Cloud RAN, explicitly designed to protect the existing installed BTS base. Nokia's MantaRay SON and energy-efficiency solutions deliver up to about 80 % operational efficiency gains and 15-30 % power savings in live networks through AI-driven optimization and multi-vendor energy management.

Nokia also pushes **Autonomous Networks**, positioning its Autonomous Networks Fabric as a cross-domain intelligence layer that unifies observability, analytics, security and automation across multi-vendor, multi-domain networks, aligned to TM Forum autonomy levels and delivered as a modular cloud/SaaS suite.

On **5G-Advanced**, Nokia defines 5G-A as an evolutionary capability layer on top of 5G that improves capacity, energy efficiency, reliability and AI-assisted operations and acts as a bridge to 6G rather than a standalone upsell wave. Features are packaged as modular software activations and pulled into vertical and mission-critical outcomes to strengthen business cases and accelerate transition to AI-native, cloud-based platforms. However, internal technical CI concludes that Ericsson's 5G-Advanced offering is broader, deeper and earlier to market across key domains such as premium performance, RedCap/IoT, device energy efficiency and differentiated connectivity, with Nokia's subscription packages materially thinner in feature content even where labels are similar.

For **mission-critical and defence**, Nokia has sharpened focus on fewer, larger high-reliability verticals such as transport, utilities, mining, manufacturing and public sector/defence, aiming for higher project values through integration and lifecycle services. The 2025 midsummer launch added assets such as the MSS-400 microwave platform, high-power microwave indoors, Habrok Massive MIMO radios, dual-band Habrok variants and the Banshee tactical 5G radio family, alongside the acquisition of a tactical communications specialist, to strengthen its mission-critical portfolio.

Nokia's **network automation and SMO** stack is centered on MantaRay SMO, positioned as a TM Forum Level-4 compliant SMO that combines network management for purpose-built and cloud RAN, application automation via MantaRay SON and rApps, and orchestration for slicing and SLA-based optimization. Nokia has further strengthened its SMO and RIC capability by licensing Juniper's RIC technology from HPE and absorbing the associated development team, with the aim of enhancing multi-vendor 5G and Open RAN automation.

Overall, Nokia's technical strengths versus Ericsson are in open and cloud RAN architecture depth, AI-enabled RAN operations, mission-critical microwave and radio portfolio and O-RAN leadership, while key weaknesses are a narrower 5G-Advanced feature set, lower network gross margins and fewer publicly proven lead-integrator vRAN/Open RAN deployments.

**Commercial and pricing behaviour**

Nokia's baseline RAN commercial strategy is structured and centrally managed. Over the last nine months of 2025 its RAN go-to-market followed a defined playbook centered on disciplined footprint defence, lifecycle positioning and multi-year transformation programs that secure long-term platform relevance and reduce contestability once they win. Pricing and negotiation behaviour is typically centralized and pricing-manager driven, with an emphasis on consistency, controlled discounting and structured commercial packaging aligned with roadmap commitments.

Within this baseline, Nokia uses Cloud RAN and 5G-Advanced narratives as strategic differentiation pillars embedded in modernization and lifecycle framing rather than as simple tactical giveaways. Monetization has shifted from tiered bundles to value-package pricing built around a mandatory main software license plus optional Value Packs, with capacity unlocked via activation keys across classic and cloud RAN. Hardware ships "dark" and is activated through hardware-activation-like license codes, supporting capex deferral and usage-aligned monetization while preserving software margin.

Under competitive pressure, Nokia departs from the baseline and becomes selectively aggressive. In high-stakes re-entry pursuits or footprint defence it has adopted materially more aggressive pricing, significant undercutting versus Ericsson, flexible commercial constructs, deferred payments, longer commitments, use of financing tools and leverage of local regulatory and investment influences to "buy relevance" or regain share. Internal case studies show examples where Nokia bid roughly 30-35 % below Ericsson in an eight-year anchor RAN modernization for a Tier-1 European operator and offered spend-based incentives and flexible deployment-linked structures, effectively bidding at cost to secure a make-or-break win. In a multi-round TCO-evaluated C-band overlay for a Tier-3 operator in MEMA Europe, Nokia tracked target TCO across five rounds, then marginally undercut Ericsson by about 0.3 % and added market-share incentives to win after challenging the procurement outcome.

Geographically, Nokia remains commercially aggressive in Africa, followed by Southeast Asia and to a lesser extent Latin America. It invests in local partnerships to broaden portfolio placement and uses sponsored programs and unconventional commercial channels. It has lost ground in the US and Japan and responds with more aggressive pricing there, while India is treated as a volume market with meagre margins.

At the price-structure level, Nokia's RAN model is primarily hardware-centric. Radios and active antennas are priced per SKU and per sector, with different SKUs by band, power class and form factor and radios sold as site capex often aligned to three-sector configurations. Capacity scaling for Massive MIMO is categorical rather than elastic, achieved by moving to higher-category hardware (for example from 32TRX to 64TRX) rather than intra-SKU software upgrades. Sectorization is monetized via licenses and activation charges so that one radio can serve multiple sectors, with additional sector functionality sold through sector licenses often paid over time and shifting some spend from capex to opex.

Overall monetization is a hybrid hardware-led framework where initial value capture is capex-driven through discrete hardware units and incremental value comes from software-enabled sector licenses, activation fees and capacity increments. Discounting is pragmatic and deal-driven: list prices are SKU-based, but final outcomes depend heavily on product volume, competitive intensity and deal-level commercial tuning across flat discounts, margin adjustments and framework structures.

Strategic analysis for EMEA notes Nokia has "slashed" 5G prices by around 10-15 % in parts of the region, funded by patent royalty cashflows of about USD 1.5 billion annually, a cost-out program of roughly USD 1 billion and approximately USD 1.5 billion in Finnvera-backed export credit, giving it a 10-15 % radio hardware cost edge via ODM sourcing. That edge is constrained by geopolitical risk, country-of-origin exposure and credit-capacity limits, particularly in NATO and EU security-sensitive segments.

**Where they win and where they lose against Ericsson**

**Where they win**

Nokia tends to win where it can combine aggressive pricing with a credible modernization and AI narrative and where operators seek lifecycle-managed, multi-year RAN transformation programs. Internal CI highlights several 2025 wins:

- Multi-year 5G-Advanced-ready modernization and expansion deals at a leading Nordic operator across Finland and the Baltics, using Habrok Massive MIMO, Pandion multiband radios and adjacent core and data-center automation to position for AI use-cases and premium 5G services.
- Extensions and new awards with a large European-African group for RAN modernization and innovation, including first dual-band Massive MIMO deployments in Africa and energy-efficiency and reliability framing.
- RAN expansions with a major Southeast Asian incumbent including ReefShark-powered AirScale portfolio and associated network management services.
- Sole-supplier continuation and extension of 5G RAN plus managed services with a leading Danish operator, where AI-driven automation and Habrok/Pandion portfolio were central to the value case.
- A three-year RAN modernization and expansion with a major Tier-1 operator in Southern Europe widely reported as a share gain at Ericsson's expense, using Habrok Massive MIMO, Pandion RRHs and extended deployment of MantaRay SON for automation and sustainability outcomes.
- A five-year nationwide modernization including Cloud RAN elements with a large Western European Tier-1 where Nokia's Cloud RAN, Habrok, Pandion, small cells and MantaRay NM formed a broad stack.

TCO analysis from recent win-loss assessments indicates Nokia often prices 15-25 % below Ericsson in Europe, roughly 10-15 % lower in parts of Asia, while being at rough parity in Africa and the Middle East in the studied deals, which aligns with its win patterns in European brownfield upgrades and some emerging markets. The commercial advisory flags Nokia's threat level as **high** for European brownfield operators, APAC emerging markets and Open RAN or multi-vendor environments, especially where its Tuuli radio and AI-automation narratives resonate with capex-constrained operators.

In mission-critical and defence, Nokia's narrowed focus on fewer, larger, high-assurance deployments and its acquisition-driven defence portfolio position it well for projects where SLA, security and lifecycle integration are valued over vendor diversity, particularly in transport, utilities and defence communications.

**Where they lose**

A notable 2025 loss was a multi-market European RAN consolidation where a large group operator awarded Ericsson a five-year renewal as exclusive RAN vendor in three European countries, which is a negative outcome for Nokia because it signals vendor consolidation around Ericsson and removes Nokia from those geographies.

In a multi-country, multi-year RAN modernization across African operations where the customer targeted a "70 % Chinese / 30 % European" vendor mix and strong capex efficiency, Nokia was incumbent across several operating companies and extremely aggressive on pricing and deferred payments but still lost the deal, indicating limits to its discount-driven approach when policy explicitly favours Chinese vendors and when local time-to-site execution and capacity-per-dollar targets dominate.

Internal advisory classifies Nokia's overall threat level as **medium** but emphasizes that its risk is concentrated in specific segments. It is most dangerous when it can frame an 8-year anchor modernization as make-or-break and is prepared to bid at or near cost with flexible incentives, and less effective where security origin rules, stringent Open RAN maturity requirements or trust and execution concerns favour Ericsson's more conservative posture.

**Position at this customer and in region**

Across **MEMA and the broader EMEA region**, Nokia's RAN install base is meaningful but generally smaller than Ericsson's, with internal data showing Ericsson leading in EMEA 5G low and mid-band node counts and Nokia holding a secondary position. Within MEMA, Nokia features in several recent RAN case studies for Tier-3 and Tier-1 European operators and for a multi-country African group modernization, as well as in Open RAN and multi-vendor environments, which signals its relevance in brownfield upgrades and multi-market programmes in and around the region.

Commercial advisory specifically flags **Open RAN and multi-vendor accounts in the Middle East**, including a leading UAE operator, as areas where Nokia's anyRAN positioning and O-RAN Alliance credibility are credible threats for 2026-2027, alongside GPU-RAN decision-makers evaluating NVIDIA-backed AI-RAN architectures.

For the unnamed leading GCC operator, the available CI sources in this folder do not document a specific major recent RAN win or loss. Instead they position Nokia as a high-threat contender in EMEA brownfield RAN modernization, multi-country African programmes and Open RAN or GPU-RAN opportunities in the Gulf, with commercial behaviour in the wider region characterized by TCO-driven bids, selective price aggression and flexible financing where share is at risk.

**Known tactics and plays**

Nokia's recurring negotiation plays show clear patterns:

- **Lifecycle lock-in and program framing.** Nokia prefers to frame RAN engagements as roadmap-anchored, multi-year transformation programs that secure long-term platform relevance and lifecycle control, reducing contestability once installed. It emphasizes modernization, AI and 5G-A/6G readiness narratives to justify long durations.
- **Centralized but flexible pricing control.** Baseline pricing is centrally controlled with pricing managers enforcing consistent discount ranges and structured packaging, yet under threat local teams can obtain approval for materially more aggressive undercutting and creative structures, including deferred payments and non-standard commitments.
- **Anchor-deal aggression.** In perceived make-or-break anchor deals, Nokia is prepared to bid approximately 30-35 % below Ericsson, effectively at cost, and to layer in spend-based incentives, deployment-linked rebates and risk-sharing constructs to secure footprint and reference value, particularly in Tier-1 European markets.
- **TCO-round gaming and procurement challenges.** In multi-round, TCO-scored tenders Nokia follows target TCO closely, then positions itself marginally below Ericsson in late rounds and has shown willingness to challenge procurement decisions procedurally to trigger extra rounds where it can adjust pricing and add market-share incentives.
- **Licensing and activation plays.** Nokia treats hardware as shipped "dark" and monetizes activation. It uses main SW plus Value Packs and activation keys for capacity, sectorization and advanced features across classic and Cloud RAN. This allows it to propose lower upfront capex while reserving software margin upside through usage-based monetization, and to design offers that align with operator capex deferral narratives.
- **Cloud RAN and 5G-A as bargaining chips.** Nokia uses Cloud RAN and 5G-Advanced features as tactical commercial levers, bundling or modularizing them based on procurement economics and customer appetite, rather than always monetizing them at full standalone value.
- **Mission-critical verticals.** In mission-critical and defence segments Nokia pursues vertical-solution GTM with verticalized sales teams, co-innovation with large enterprises, systems integrators and hyperscalers and higher-value project-driven models with bespoke integration and outcome-based or hybrid commercial constructs.
- **Risk-sharing and unconventional channels.** In some regions it is willing to explore revenue-share or success-based pricing, invest in local partnerships to open unconventional channels and align with local sponsored programs, especially in Africa and parts of Asia, which can give it political and ecosystem leverage beyond the RAN bill of materials.

These plays make Nokia particularly dangerous in anchor tenders, TCO-based evaluations and multi-year modernization programmes where commercial creativity and lifecycle framing can offset technical gaps.

**How Ericsson counters them**

The Ericsson strategic response recommends a **three-layer playbook**: **Defend, Equalise, Monetise.**

- **Defend with secure-supply and Western origin.** Ericsson should weaponize its roughly 85 % Western bill of materials and NATO "trusted supplier" status to justify an 8-12 % price premium in defence and critical-infrastructure segments. This includes independent origin audits, lobbying for origin-rule labelling in EU/NATO tenders and bundling cyber-assurance SLAs and incident-response options into offers. This directly targets Nokia's reliance on Chinese and Vietnamese ODM production and higher country-of-origin exposure.
- **Equalise via open-ecosystem cost parity.** To neutralize Nokia's roughly 10-15 % hardware cost edge from low-cost ODMs, Ericsson should execute an O-RAN-ready cost-out program using EU/US merchant silicon, Central and Eastern European final assembly and modular mechanical design, targeting a macro-radio COGS index reduction from 100 to about 82 by 2028, i.e. roughly 90 % of Nokia's forecast cost. This keeps hardware price deltas manageable, prevents destructive price matching and protects future software profit pools attached to the installed base. Financing counter-measures such as EKN/EIB-backed vendor finance can neutralize Finnvera's credit advantage without cutting list prices.
- **Monetise via AI-platform and SaaS.** Ericsson should accelerate its RIC and rApp ecosystem and launch tiered 5G SaaS offers for slice management and AI-OPS, targeting a step-up in ARR and high-margin software pools by 2028-2029. This shifts the fight to software, security and platform trust where Nokia is structurally weaker and more dependent on hardware discounts and patent cashflows.

Game-theory modelling suggests Ericsson should keep competitive dynamics in zones where Nokia's discounting remains moderate and Ericsson's strategic aggressiveness is high, avoiding the "price-war valley" where both sides escalate aggressively. Practically this means using security premiums, cost-parity hardware, selective financing and platform upsell to deter extreme Nokia undercutting rather than matching headline discounts.

The Nokia commercial-strategy CI adds pragmatic guidance:

- Compete on **programme logic and value framing** rather than BoQ alone. Ericsson should leverage trusted CSP relationships to shape requirements early, create clear "change triggers" with quantified TCO, energy and operations benefits and force feature-level comparisons, especially in 5G-Advanced, to avoid false equivalence between thinner Nokia subscription packages and richer Ericsson offers.
- Exploit **Nokia's perceived rigidity and trust deficits** in complex modernizations by emphasizing Ericsson's commercial clarity, responsiveness and delivery certainty during late-stage negotiations.
- Use **selective participation**. For ultra-low-margin macro deployments where Nokia or Chinese vendors drive TCO to levels that would erode Ericsson margins even at cost parity, Ericsson should be prepared not to chase and instead focus on segments where security, performance, automation and lifecycle outcomes sustain a premium.

**Recent moves and watch-items**

Key recent moves and watch items from the internal material:

- **Organizational and strategic pivot.** Nokia simplified from four business groups to two operating segments effective 1 Jan 2026, added a Technology and AI Organisation and Corporate Development Organisation and unified its customer interface under "One Nokia," anchoring strategy on AI-native networks and 6G. This increases its ability to industrialize AI/security assets and sharpen capital allocation.
- **AI-RAN and NVIDIA partnership.** The October 2025 strategic partnership in which NVIDIA invests about USD 1 billion and co-develops ARC-Pro for AI-RAN is central to Nokia's messaging to GPU-RAN decision-makers and positions Nokia to turn cell sites into edge AI platforms in 5G/6G timelines.
- **Midsummer 2025 product wave.** New microwave, Massive MIMO, dual-band radios, predictive software analytics and MantaRay SMO upgrades reinforce Nokia's narrative of 5G-Advanced readiness, monetization-oriented value packages and a path to autonomous networks and 6G.
- **Mission-critical and defence push.** The acquisition of a tactical communications player and launches like the Mission-Safe Phone and Banshee 5G Tactical Radio show a deliberate expansion into defence and public-safety communications, aligned with mission-critical vertical growth priorities.
- **Open RAN and anyRAN execution.** Nokia continues to invest in O-RAN leadership, ULPI uplink performance improvements and multi-supplier implementations, including deployment with multiple third-party radios and phased Cloud RAN plus O-RAN rollouts. This strengthens its credibility in any future Open RAN or multi-vendor RAN decisions at EMEA operators, including in the Gulf.
- **AI and fibre/network automation.** Nokia is investing in fixed-network R&D (for example its large Chennai facility) and AI-driven tools such as FTTH digital twins and Altiplano-based fibre health analyzers and is expanding fibre broadband in underserved markets, which strengthens its end-to-end positioning in broadband plus 5G.
- **China market access risk.** China has tightened restrictions on Nokia and Ericsson with "black box" national-security reviews and curbs on their equipment in domestic networks, driving their combined market share in the Chinese mobile network market to low single digits and posing a structural revenue and scale risk that Nokia is actively lobbying the EU to counter via reciprocal policies against high-risk vendors.
- **Financial trajectory.** 2025 and Q4 results show volatile margins with Mobile Networks as the main swing factor, but strong seasonal improvements when mix and cost savings align. Nokia expects multi-year growth from fibre and advanced 5G/6G deployments, while acknowledging ongoing pressure in Mobile Networks and mixed EPS trends.

For Ericsson's engagement with a leading GCC operator, the watch-items are Nokia's selective use of very deep discounting in anchor bids, its anyRAN plus NVIDIA AI-RAN positioning in Open RAN and GPU-RAN discussions, its mission-critical and defence portfolio expansion and its aggressive push to package AI and 5G-Advanced as monetizable value packs even where feature depth is behind Ericsson.
